meta.manuscript_location: Shrine of the Book, Israel Museum, Jerusalem

preamble.summary: Isaiah 47 is a taunt song against Babylon — 'Come down and sit in the dust, O virgin daughter of Babylon.' The chapter has 15 verses spanning columns XXXVIII-XXXIX. The scroll shows mostly orthographic variants with one moderate reading.
preamble.notable_variants: Verse 5: the scroll has a minor variant in 'mistress of kingdoms.' Verse 10: a moderate variant in the pronoun suffix. The dramatic poetry of Babylon's fall is otherwise identical in both traditions.

variant_notes: No significant variant. The scroll reads identically to the MT here.

Identical consonantal reading. 1QIsaiah-a preserves the title 'mistress of kingdoms' for Babylon without variant. The dramatic irony — Babylon was called mistress but will be brought to nothing — is the same in both texts.
No significant variant. The scroll reads identically to the MT here.

1QIsaiah-a uses plene spelling with vav and a different suffix form (-ki instead of -ekh). This is a characteristic Qumran morphological feature for the second-person feminine singular. No change in meaning: 'Your wisdom and your knowledge led you astray.'
No significant variant. The scroll reads identically to the MT here.

Plene spelling. Same word — reference to Babylon's astrologers and sorcerers.
